docs.title: Metric Space
docs.text:
Definition 1.1: Metric space

A metric space is a pair \((M, d)\) consisting of a set of points \(M\) and a metric \(d: M \times M \to \mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}\). \(d\) is symmetric, positive-definite, and satisfies the triangle-inequality.

Example 1.1: \(\mathbb{R}^N\)

  1. The real line \(\mathbb{R}\) is a metric space under \(d(x, y) = |x - y|\).

  2. \(\mathbb{R}^2\) is a metric space where \(d((x_1, y_1), (x_2, y_2)) = \sqrt{(x_1 - y_1)^2 + (x_2 - y_2)^2}\).

This metric allows for very natural definitions of convergence and continuity.

Definition 1.2: Convergence

Let \((x_n)_{n \geq 1}\) be a sequence of points in a metric space \((M, d)\). We say that \(x_n\) converges to \(x\) if for all \(\epsilon > 0\), there is an integer \(N_{\epsilon}\) such that for each \(n \geq N_{\epsilon}\),

\[ d(x_n, x) < \epsilon \]

Definition 1.3: Continuity

Let \(M = (M, d_M), N = (N, d_N)\) be metric spaces. A function \(f: M \to N\) is continuous at a point \(p \in M\) if for every \(\epsilon > 0\), there exists a \(\delta > 0\) such that:

\[ d_M(x, p) < \epsilon \implies d_N(f(x), f(p)) < \delta \]

An equivalent condition is that of sequential continuity. A function \(f: M \to N\) is continuous at \(p \in M\) if and only if the following property holds: if \(x_1, x_2, \dots\) is a sequence in \(M\) converging to \(p\), then the sequence \(f(x_1), f(x_2), \dots\) in \(N\) converges to \(f(p)\).

Definition 1.4: Bounded

A metric space \(M\) is bounded iff for every point \(p \in M\), there is a radius \(R\) such that for all $q \in $M, $d(p, q) \leq $.

Prototypical example: \([0, 1]\) is bounded but \(\R\) is not.

docs.title: Topology
docs.text:
Topological space define notions like continuity, open, and closed without a metric. A metric naturally defines open sets but we can start with the open sets andn forget the metric entirely.

Definition 2.1: Topological space

A topological space is a pair \((X, \mathcal{T})\) where \(X\) is a set of points and \(\mathcal{T}\) is the topology which consists of subsets of \(X\) called the open sets of \(X\). Specifically,

  1. \(\emptyset, X \in \mathcal{T}\).
  2. Finite intersectsions of open sets are in \(\mathcal{T}\).
  3. Arbitrary unions of open sets are in \(\mathcal{T}\).

Definition 2.2: Open Neighborhood

An open neighborhood of a point \(x \in X\) is an open set \(U\) which contains \(x\).

Definition 2.3: Continuity

A function \(f: X \to Y\) of topological spaces is continuous at \(p \in X\) if the pre-image of any open neighborhood of \(f(p)\) contains an open-neighborhood of \(p\).

Example 2.1: Hausdorff space

A topological space \(X\) is Hausdorff if for any two distinct points \(p, q \in X\), there exist an open neighborhood \(U\) of \(p\) and \(V\) of \(q\) which do not intersect.

An intuition behind Hausdorff spaces is that these spaces have resolution - we can always zoom in to "tell points apart". All metric spaces are Hausdorff. Consider two distinct points \(x, y\) where \(d(x, y) = \epsilon, \epsilon > 0\). We can draw \(\epsilon / 4\)-neighborhoods around \(x, y\) which do not intersect. Hence, any metric space is Hausdorff.

Compactness generalizes the idea of being closed and bounded in a topological space.

Definition 2.4: Open Cover

An open cover of a topological space \(X\) is a collection of open sets \(\{U_{\alpha}\}\) which covers it.

\[ X = \bigcup U_{\alpha} \]

A sub-cover is a subset of \(U_{\alpha}\) which still overs \(X\).

Note that equality comes from the "universe" being \(X\).

Definition 2.5: Compact

A topological space \(X\) is quasicompact if every open cover has a finite subcover. It is compact if it is also Hausdorff.

docs.title: Measures
docs.text:
Consider a non-empty set \(\Omega\).

Definition 3.1: \(\sigma\)-algebra

A collection \(\mathcal{S}\) of subsets of \(\Omega\) for which:

  1. \(\Omega \in \mathcal{S}\).
  2. \(A \in \mathcal{S} \implies A^C \in \mathcal{S}\).
  3. \(S_1, S_2, \dots \in \mathcal{S} \implies S_1 \cup S_2 \cup \dots \in \mathcal{S}\).

The pair \((\Omega, \mathcal{S})\) is called a measurable space.

Definition 3.2: Measure

Let \(S\) be a \(\sigma\)-algebra on \(\Omega\). A map \(\mu: S \to [0, \infty]\) is called a measure if:

  1. \(\mu(\emptyset) = 0\).
  2. \(S_1, S_2, \dots,\) are pairwise-disjoint implies that \(\mu(S_1 \cup S_1 \cup \dots) = \sum_{i} \mu(S_i)\).

The \(\sigma\)-algebra selects which events \(S \subset \Omega\) we can measure. For the trivial \(\sigma\)-algebra, \(\{\emptyset, \Omega\}\), all we know is that \(\mu(\emptyset) = 0, \mu(\Omega) = 1\).

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
What kind of models should we prefer? Do we prefer simplistic models as is in line with Occam's Razor? Do we prefer exceedingly complex models?

We argue that we should prefer highly flexible models with inductive biases which determine how we distribute our a priori support.

Note: Model and model class are used interchangeably below.

Probability Rules:

  1. Sum Rule:

    \[\begin{equation} p(A) = \int_B p(A \mid B) \ p(B) \ dB \end{equation}\]

    Again,

    \[\begin{equation} p(A, B) = \int_C p(A, B \mid C) \ p(C) \ dC \end{equation}\]

  2. Product Rule:

    \[\begin{equation} p(A, B) = p(A \mid B) p(B) \end{equation}\]

docs.title: Model Construction and Statistical Inference
docs.text:
Often times machine learning is treating as a grab-bag of seemingly unrelated techniques: supervised learning, unsupervised learning, dimensionality reduction, etc. The differences between these techniques are trivial from a probabilistic perspective. Our approach for all of these problems is very simple. We begin with some dataset \(\mathcal{D}\).

This dataset may be comprised of input-output pairs. For example, we may have \(\mathcal{D} = \{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i = 1}^N\). This called supervised learning.

This dataset can also just be comprised of single data points, i.e. \(\mathcal{D} = \{x_i\}_{i = 1}^N\). The problem of finding the probability distribution which generated \(\mathcal{D}\) is called density estimation. This is an example of unsupervised learning but is only trivially different from supervised learning when this probabilistic approach is taken.

In each scenario, we begin by assuming a causal story for how our data was generated by specifying an observation model. In Bayes Rule, we have:

\[ p(\theta \mid \mathcal{D}) = \frac{p(\mathcal{D} \mid \theta) p(\theta)}{p(\mathcal{D})} \]

Here \(\theta\) (occasionally written as \(a, w\)) are the parameters of our observation model. For example, if we assume a linear observation model, we have:

\[ y_i = a x_i + \epsilon_x \]

where \(\epsilon_x\) is some irreducible noise term.

The process of inferring parameters like \(\theta\) is called . This can be done by finding the joint distribution, \(p(D, \theta)\). Once we have the joint distribution, through the sum and product rules, we can recover the marginal and conditional distribution.

\[ p(D) = \int p(D \mid \theta) p(\theta) d\theta $$ $$ p(D \mid \theta) = \frac{p(D, \theta)}{p(\theta)} \]

Bayesian inference allows us to faithfully represent our uncertainty over the parameters \(\theta\). Even if we find a point estimate \(\hat{\theta}\) which maximizes the likelihood of the data given \(\theta\), i.e. \(p(\mathcal{D} \mid \theta)\), given a limited amount of data, we still have uncertainty over the true value of \(\theta\).

docs.title: MLE
docs.text:
MLE or Maximum Likelihood Estimation is a procedure for inferring the parameters of some model.

Let \(\mathcal{D} = \{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i = 1}^n\). We begin by specifying an observation model that relates \(y_i\) and \(x_i\) through some function \(f(x, a) = ax\).

::: centeredboxgreen Model Classes

The observation model is generally a - specific models within this model class are specified by some parameter value \(\theta\). We always assume that the ground truth is an element of this model class, i.e. \(y_i\) and \(x_i\) truly do have some linear relationship. For simplicity's sake, we 're using a linear model but in reality, we often want much more flexible models. We will examine this in further detail in the model selection section. :::

So we have: $\(\begin{equation} \label{eq:obs_1} y = ax + \epsilon_x \end{equation}\)$ where \(\epsilon_x \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)\) is our Gaussian noise term. We want to know how likely it is that our model could have generated this dataset. Specifically, the is defined as: $\(\begin{equation} \theta_{\text{ML}} = \mathop{\mathrm{arg\,max}}_{\theta} p(\mathcal{D} \mid \theta) \end{equation}\)$

We begin by computing the likelihood that the data was generated by our model, i.e. \(p(\mathcal{D} \mid \theta)\). Since the only random term in \((\ref{eq:obs_1})\) is \(\epsilon_x\), the random variable \(y\)'s distribution is just a displaced version of \(\epsilon_x\)'s distribution. We have:

\[\begin{equation} y \mid a, x, \sigma^2 \sim \mathcal{N}(y ; ax, \sigma^2) \end{equation}$$ $$\begin{equation} p(y \mid x, a, \sigma^2) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2 \pi \sigma^2}} \exp \left \{ -\frac{1}{2 \sigma^2} (y - ax)^2 \right \} \end{equation}\]

However, this is the likelihood of a single sample \((x_i, y_i)\). We want the likelihood of the entire dataset. Let \(\mathbf{y} = (y_1, \dots, y_n)^T, \mathbf{X} = (x_1, \dots, x_N)^T\). We can assume that \(\mathbf{y}\) is conditionally independent on the parameters \(a, \sigma^2\).

:::: centeredboxblue ::: example Example: 1. Conditional Independence

Suppose we have a normal distribution \(\mathcal{N}(\mu, 1)\). The first sample I draw from this distribution has value \(1000000\). Does this influence your beliefs about the second sample I will draw? It should. Clearly, sample 1 and 2 are not completely independent.

If the distribution is \(\mathcal{N}(1000001, 1)\) and the first sample I draw is \(1000000\), does that influence your beliefs about the second sample? It shouldn't really. These samples are conditionally independent once the parameters of the distribution are known. ::: ::::

We want to find the likelihood of the entire dataset, \(p(y_1, \dots y_n \mid x_1, \dots, x_n, \sigma^2, a) = p(\mathbf{y} \mid \mathbf{x}, a, \sigma^2)\). We have: $\(\begin{align} p(\mathbf{y} \mid a, \mathbf{X}, \sigma^2) &= \prod_{i = 1}^n p(y_i \mid a, x_i, \sigma^2) \\ &= \prod_{i = 1}^n \frac{1}{\sqrt{2 \pi \sigma^2}} \exp \left \{ - \frac{1}{2 \sigma^2} (y_i - ax_i)^2 \right \} \\ &= \frac{1}{(2 \pi \sigma^2)^{\frac{n}{2}}} \exp \left \{ - \frac{1}{2 \sigma^2} \sum_{i = 1}^n (y_i^2 - 2 a x_i y_i + a^2 x_i^2) \right \} \\ &= \frac{1}{(2 \pi \sigma^2)^{\frac{n}{2}}} \exp \left \{ - \frac{1}{2 \sigma^2} (\mathbf{y}^T \mathbf{y} - 2 a \mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{y} + a^2 \mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{x}) \right \} \end{align}\)$ A common trick is to take the log-likelihood (this could've been done earlier to convert the product to a sum but this also works). $\(\begin{align} \log p(\mathbf{y} \mid a, \mathbf{X}, \sigma^2) = - \frac{n}{2} \log (2 \pi) - \frac{n}{2} \log (\sigma^2) - \frac{1}{2\sigma^2} (\mathbf{y}^T \mathbf{y} - 2 a \mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{y} + a^2 \mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{x}) \end{align}\)$ Now, by taking derivatives we can find maximum likelihood estimators. $\(\begin{align} 0 = \frac{\partial \log p(\mathbf{y} \mid a, \mathbf{X}, \sigma^2)}{\partial a} &= \frac{d}{da} 2 a \mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{y} - \frac{d}{da} a^2 \mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{x} \\ 2 a \mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{x} &= 2 \mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{y} \\ \Aboxed{a_{\text{ML}} &= \frac{\mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{y}}{\mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{x}}} \end{align}\)$ $\(\begin{align} 0 = \frac{\partial \log p(\mathbf{y} \mid a, \mathbf{X}, \sigma^2)}{\partial \sigma^2} &= - \frac{n}{2 \sigma^2} + \frac{1}{2 \sigma^4} (\mathbf{y} - a \mathbf{x})^2 \\ \Aboxed{\sigma^2_{\text{ML}} &= \frac{1}{n} (\mathbf{y} - a_{\text{ML}} \mathbf{x})^2} \end{align}\)$

:::: centeredboxgreen ::: intuition Intuition Check: 1. Likelihood

The likelihood, \(p(D \mid a)\) is the probability of observing some data points \(D\) given a parameter setting \(a\). The maximum likelihood estimators are the values which maximize this, i.e. \(\hat{a} = \mathop{\mathrm{arg\,max}}_a p(D \mid a)\). Intuitively, if we set our parameters to a specific \(a_0\) and our likelihood decreases, we know \(a_0\) is unlikely to be the correct parameter setting (or equivalent to \(a\) in the observation model). Maximizing likelihood is a frequentist method but isn't always the correct solution. ::: ::::

The MLE for the coefficient in the linear regression problem is the standard result - \(\hat{a} = \frac{\text{Cov}(X, y)}{\mathop{\mathrm{Var}}(X)} = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T y\) . The MLE for the variance of the noise term is the mean-squared-error - MSE can be written as the sum of bias + variance and since the bias of the error term is \(0\) (as it has \(0\) mean), the MSE is directly equivalent to the variance \(\sigma^2\).

:::: centeredboxgreen ::: intuition Intuition Check: 2. Likelihood

The likelihood can be written as \(p(\mathbf{y} \mid \mathbf{x}) = \prod_{i = 1}^n p(y_i \mid x_i)\) ::: ::::

docs.title: MAP
docs.text:
Maximizing \(p(D \mid a)\) as the likelihood is maximizing the probability of observing \(D\) given \(a\). But we care about setting our parameters correctly, not just observing \(D\). Maximum a posteriori estimation is about maximizing the posterior probability, or the probability of the parameters given the data. Specifically, the MAP is:

\[\hat{a} = \mathop{\mathrm{arg\,max}}_a p(a | D) \propto \mathop{\mathrm{arg\,max}}_a p(D | a) p(a)$$ $$= \mathop{\mathrm{arg\,max}}_a \log p(a | D) = \mathop{\mathrm{arg\,max}}_a \log p(D | a) + \mathop{\mathrm{arg\,max}}_a \log p(a)\]

If we place a prior distribution over \(a \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \gamma^2)\), then we have: $\(p(a \mid \gamma^2, \mathbf{y}, X, \sigma^2) = p(\mathbf{y} \mid X, \sigma^2, a) \ p(a \mid \gamma^2)\)$ $\(\log p(a \mid D, \gamma^2) = \log p(\mathbf{y} \mid X, \sigma^2, a) + \log p(a \mid \gamma^2)\)$

We know the form of the log-likelihood as derived above. $\(\log p(a \mid \gamma^2) = \log \left ( \frac{1}{\sqrt{2 \pi \gamma^2}} \exp \left \{ - \frac{1}{2 \gamma^2} a^2 \right \} \right )\)$ $\(= - \frac{1}{2} \log (2\pi \gamma^2) - \frac{a^2}{2 \gamma^2}\)$ So the log-posterior is: $\(\log p(a \mid D, \gamma^2) = - \frac{n}{2} \log (2 \pi \sigma^2) - \frac{1}{2 \sigma^2}(\mathbf{y} - a \mathbf{x})^2 - \frac{1}{2} \log (2 \pi \gamma^2) - \frac{a^2}{2 \gamma^2}\)$ $\(\hat{a}_{\text{MAP}} = \mathop{\mathrm{arg\,min}}_a \frac{1}{2 \sigma^2} ((y - a \mathbf{x})^2 + \frac{\sigma^2}{\gamma^2} a^2)\)$ $\(= \mathop{\mathrm{arg\,min}}_a (y - a \mathbf{x})^2 + \lambda a^2\)$

:::: centeredboxred ::: warning Warning: 1. MAP is not Bayesian

We've essentially recovered ridge regression from assuming Gaussian noise with a Gaussian prior. We can also recover Lasso regression from assuming Laplace noise with a Laplace prior. However, MAP Estimation is not a Bayesian method. The idea behind Bayesian inference is to construct a probability distribution over the set of possible values for \(a\). MAP does not marginalize over the prior distribution so it does not constitute a fully Bayesian treatment. ::: ::::

docs.title: Bayesian Inference
docs.text:
1. Prior: We begin with some prior distribution \(p(a)\). We have some dataset \(D\) so we can compute the likelihood \(p(D \mid a)\) to evaluate how well a particular setting of \(a\) matches the dataset (the probability of observing \(D\) given \(a\)).

  2. Posterior: We can then compute the posterior distribution. $\(p(a \mid D) \propto p(D \mid A) \ p(a)\)$

  3. Bayesian Model Average: Now, given our updated beliefs, \(p(a \mid D)\), we want to compute the predictive distribution. Take our linear regression example - we have some dataset \(D\) which you might think of as the training dataset. Now we have a new point \(x_*\) and want to use our beliefs to construct a predictive distribution for \(y_*\). In computing the predictive distribution, we again marginalize over the parameter \(a\). $\(p(y_* \mid x_*, \hat{\sigma}^2, \hat{\gamma}^2, \mathbf{y}, X) = \int_a p(y_*, a \mid x_*, \hat{\sigma}^2, \hat{\gamma}^2, \mathbf{y}, X) \ da\)$ $\(= \int_a p(y_* \mid x_*, a, \hat{\sigma}^2) \ p(a \mid \hat{\gamma}^2, \mathbf{y}, X) \ da\)$

    The Bayesian Model Average essentially weights all of the predictions of \(y_*\) with some weight setting \(a\) by the posterior probability of \(a\). The posterior probability is the probability of \(a\) after having observed the data (i.e. how well \(a\) matches the data).

:::: centeredboxgreen ::: intuition Intuition Check: 3. Marginalizing over the Posterior

Observe that when we were marginalizing over the data to obtain the model evidence, we marginalized over the prior distribution (so each likelihood was being weighted by our prior belief in it). When we marginalize over \(a\) now, we're marginalizing over the posterior distribution. Now, each value of \(y_*\) conditional on some value of \(a\) is weighted by our updated belief in it, derived from the training data \(D\).

The idea with Bayesian Model Averaging is to not bet everything on a single \(a\) but to consider all possible parameter settings \(a\) and also to assume that the parameters are not completely determined by the data. ::: ::::

Generally, as the number of samples observed in \(D\) increases, the posterior distribution becomes increasingly sharp, centered around the true parameter value \(\hat{a}\). This can be seen in the following diagram from Bishop (3.7). You can view the multi-variate Gaussian describing the prior / posterior as concentrating and sharpening around the true parameter value. Sequentially, we begin with a prior, derive a posterior and the posterior becomes the new prior - this is the online nature of sequential Bayesian learning.

Posterior Concentration

Bayesian Inference is the process of determining the posterior and thus, the Bayesian Model Average.

:::: centeredboxblue ::: example Example: 2. Frequentist vs Bayesian Urns

Suppose we have \(11\) urns \(u_{[0, \dots, 10]}\). Each urn has \(10\) balls. The \(i\)th urn has \(i\) black balls and \(10 - i\) white balls. So the first urn has \(0\) black balls, the \(10\)th urn has \(10\) black balls.

You are blindfolded and are given an urn. You draw \(10\) balls with replacement from the urn, one at a time. \(3\) balls are black and \(7\) balls are white. What is the probability of the next ball being black? Specifically, is the probability greater than, equal to, or less than \(30 \%\)?

The MLE solution is to say that you are in the \(3\)rd urn and that the probability is equal to \(30\%\). However, this doesn't incorporate uncertainty about the fact that you may be in the \(4\)th or \(5\)th urn. This uncertainty is included in the Bayesian Model Average. In reality, the probability of the next ball being black is around \(34\%\). ::: ::::

:::: centeredboxred ::: warning Warning: 2. MLE isn't necessarily optimal

Gaussian Mixture Models are extremely expressive - they're dense in the set of probability distributions and a mixture of \(4\) or \(5\) Gaussians is sufficient to model most classical statistical settings.

Specifically, we can gain infinite likelihood with a mixture of two Gaussians. Note that this is density estimation so we're just predicting \(X\), rather than \(y\) from \(X\). $\(p(D \mid \mu_1, \mu_2, \sigma_1^2, \sigma_2^2) = \prod_{i = 1}^n \frac{w_1}{\sqrt{2 \pi \sigma_1^2}} \exp \left \{ - \frac{1}{2 \sigma_1^2} (x_i - \mu_1)^2 \right \} + \frac{w_2}{\sqrt{2 \pi \sigma_2^2}} \exp \left \{ - \frac{1}{2 \sigma_2^2} (x_i - \mu_2)^2 \right \}\)$

By setting \(\mu_1 = x_j\) and \(\sigma_1^2 \to 0\), we can get essentially infinite likelihood for \(x_j\). The other Gaussian provides probability mass to the other points to make this the product of infinity with other non-zero numbers to get essentially infinite likelihood. This doesn't mean that the model fits the data well - in fact, it fits it extremely poorly. This is an example of MLE overfitting. To solve this, models will often be re-initialized with the hope of falling into a local minimum rather than a global minimum. This suggests that MLE is not a good objective function at all for this specific case. ::: ::::

docs.title: Marginal Likelihood
docs.text:
There are \(3\) different ways to approach any ML problem. In each case, we have some function \(f(x, w)\) that we want to estimate by inferring information about \(w\).

  1. Directly specific some loss function \(L\) and minimize it with respect to \(w\).

  2. Create a probabilistic story about how the data was generated (observation model) to form a likelihood \(p(D \mid w)\) and maximize this with respect to \(w\) (MLE or MAP).

  3. Take the Bayesian Approach. Compute the Bayesian Model Average, integrating over all possible parameter settings, weighted by their posterior probabilities for the predictive distribution.

Approaches \(1\) and \(2\) are very similar but the full Bayesian approach is very different. This function is very flexible - \(f(x, w)\) can be done for regression / classification, unsupervised learning (as density estimation) and the procedure for doing these is only trivially different.

In Bayesian Linear Regression, consider the general form of a Linear Gaussian Model: $\(y = \mathbf{w}^T \phi(x) + \epsilon(x)\)$ $\(\epsilon(x) \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)\)$ $\(\mathbf{w} \mid \alpha \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \alpha^2 I)\)$

Here, \(\phi(x)\) is a vector of basis functions (for example, \(\phi(x) = [1, x, x^2, \dots, x^P]^T\)). Clearly, \(y(x)\) would be a non-linear function but would be linear in \(w\), the parameter we're trying to estimate so we still consider this a linear model.

For a dataset \(\mathcal{D} = \{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i = 1}^n\), we want to find the marginal likelihood, \(p(\mathbf{y} \mid X, \sigma^2, \alpha^2)\). This is the denominator in Bayes equation, also known as the partition function.

First, we marginalize over \(\mathbf{w}\). $\(\begin{equation} p(\mathbf{y} \mid X, \sigma^2, \alpha^2) = \int p(\mathbf{y} \mid \mathbf{w}, X, \sigma^2) p(\mathbf{w} \mid \alpha) \ d\mathbf{w} \end{equation}\)$

The prior and likelihood are both normal: $\(\begin{equation} = \int \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{w} ; 0, \alpha^2 I) \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{y} ; \bm{\Phi} \mathbf{w}, \sigma^2 I) \ d\mathbf{w} \end{equation}\)$ $\(\begin{equation} = c \int \exp \left \{- \frac{1}{2} \left ( \frac{\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{w}}{\alpha^2} + \frac{(\mathbf{y} - \bm{\Phi} \mathbf{w})^T (\mathbf{y} - \bm{\Phi} \mathbf{w})}{\sigma^2} \right ) \right \} \ d\mathbf{w} \end{equation}\)$

\[\begin{equation} = c \exp \left \{ - \frac{1}{2} \left [ \frac{\bm{w}^T \bm{w}}{\alpha^2} + \frac{1}{\sigma^2} (\bm{y}^T \bm{y} + \bm{w}^T \bm{\Phi}^T \bm{\Phi} \bm{w} - 2 \bm{w}^T \bm{\Phi}^T \bm{y}) \right ] \right \} \end{equation}\] \[\begin{equation} \label{eq:lambda} = c \exp \left \{ - \frac{1}{2} \left [ \bm{w}^T (\frac{I}{\alpha^2} + \frac{\bm{\Phi}^T \bm{\Phi}}{\sigma^2}) \bm{w} + \frac{\bm{y}^T \bm{y}}{\sigma^2} - 2 \frac{\bm{w}^T \bm{\Phi}^T \bm{y}}{\sigma^2} \right ] \right \} \end{equation}\]

Write the precision matrix, \(\Lambda = \frac{I}{\alpha^2} + \frac{\bm{\Phi}^T \bm{\Phi}}{\sigma^2}\).

\[\begin{equation} \label{eq:5} = c' \exp \left \{ - \frac{1}{2} \left [ \bm{w}^T \Lambda \bm{w} - 2 \frac{\bm{w}^T \bm{\Phi}^T \bm{y}}{\sigma^2} \right ] \right \} \end{equation}\]

Now we can really complete the square. First, we need to figure out the mean vector. We have:

\[\begin{equation} (\bm{w} - \bm{\mu})^T \Lambda (\bm{w} - \bm{\mu}) = \bm{w}^T \Lambda \bm{w} + \bm{\mu}^T \Lambda \bm{\mu} - 2 \bm{w}^T \Lambda \bm{\mu} \end{equation}\]

By inspection, we have: $\(\begin{equation} \Lambda \bm{\mu} = \frac{\bm{\Phi}^T \bm{y}}{\sigma^2} \end{equation}\)$ $\(\begin{equation} \bm{\mu} = \Lambda^{-1} \frac{\bm{\Phi}^T \bm{y}}{\sigma^2} \end{equation}\)$

Substituting in our mean vector, we have:

\[\begin{equation} (\bm{w} - \bm{\mu})^T \Lambda (\bm{w} - \bm{\mu}) = \bm{w}^T \Lambda \bm{w} + \frac{\bm{y}^T \bm{\Phi}}{\sigma^2} \Lambda^{-1} \Lambda \Lambda^{-1} \frac{\bm{\Phi}^T \bm{y}}{\sigma^2} - 2 \bm{w}^T \Lambda \Lambda^{-1} \frac{\bm{\Phi}^T \bm{y}}{\sigma^2} \end{equation}\] \[\begin{equation} = \bm{w}^T \Lambda \bm{w} + \frac{\bm{y}^T \bm{\Phi}}{\sigma^2} \Lambda^{-1} \frac{\bm{\Phi}^T \bm{y}}{\sigma^2} - 2 \bm{w}^T \frac{\bm{\Phi}^T \bm{y}}{\sigma^2} \end{equation}$$ $$\begin{equation} (\bm{w} - \bm{\mu})^T \Lambda (\bm{w} - \bm{\mu}) - \frac{\bm{y}^T \bm{\Phi}}{\sigma^2} \Lambda^{-1} \frac{\bm{\Phi}^T \bm{y}}{\sigma^2} = \bm{w}^T \Lambda \bm{w} - 2 \bm{w}^T \frac{\bm{\Phi}^T \bm{y}}{\sigma^2} \end{equation}\]

Substituting in and putting the product of our MVGs back into the integral, we have:

\[\begin{equation} p(\bm{y} \mid X, \sigma^2, \alpha^2, \bm{z}) = c' \int \exp \left \{ - \frac{1}{2} \left [ (\bm{w} - \bm{\mu})^T \Lambda (\bm{w} - \bm{\mu}) - \frac{\bm{y}^T \bm{\Phi}}{\sigma^2} \Lambda^{-1} \frac{\bm{\Phi}^T \bm{y}}{\sigma^2} \right ] \right \} \end{equation}\] \[\begin{equation} = c'' \int \exp \left \{ - \frac{1}{2} (\bm{w} - \bm{\mu})^T \Lambda (\bm{w} - \bm{\mu}) \right \} d\bm{w} \end{equation}\]

The exponential term is the unnormalized version of \(\mathcal{N}(\bm{w} ; \bm{\mu}, \Lambda^{-1})\). Since the integral over \(\bm{w}\) must be normalized, this must integrate to the normalizing coefficient or:

\[\begin{equation} \int \exp \left \{ - \frac{1}{2} (\bm{w} - \bm{\mu})^T \Lambda (\bm{w} - \bm{\mu}) \right \} d\bm{w} = \frac{(2 \pi)^{\frac{D}{2}}}{\det (\Lambda)^{\frac{1}{2}}} \end{equation}\]

So we get: $\(\begin{equation} p(\bm{y} \mid X, \sigma^2, \alpha^2, \bm{z}) = c'' \frac{(2 \pi)^{\frac{D}{2}}}{\det (\Lambda)^{\frac{1}{2}}} \end{equation}\)$

\[\begin{equation} p(\bm{y} \mid X, \sigma^2, \alpha^2, \bm{z}) = \frac{\exp \left \{- \frac{1}{2} \left [ \frac{\bm{y}^T \bm{y}}{\sigma^2} - \frac{\bm{y}^T \bm{\Phi}}{\sigma^2} \Lambda^{-1} \frac{\bm{\Phi}^T \bm{y}}{\sigma^2} \right ] \right \}}{(2 \pi \sigma^2)^\frac{n}{2} \det (\Lambda)^{\frac{1}{2}} (\alpha^2)^{\frac{D}{2}}} \end{equation}\] \[\begin{equation} \label{eq:e7} = \frac{ \exp \left \{ - \frac{1}{2} \bm{y}^T \left [ \frac{I}{\sigma^2} - \frac{ \bm{\Phi}}{\sigma^2} \Lambda^{-1} \frac{\bm{\Phi}^T}{\sigma^2} \right ] \bm{y} \right \} }{(2 \pi \sigma^2)^\frac{n}{2} \det (\Lambda)^{\frac{1}{2}} (\alpha^2)^{\frac{D}{2}}} \end{equation}\]

Define \(\Lambda_0 = \frac{1}{\sigma^2} I - \frac{1}{\sigma^4} \bm{\Phi} \Lambda^{-1} \bm{\Phi}^T\). Then, the numerator for [eq:e7]{reference-type="ref" reference="eq:e7"} becomes the exponential for the unnormalized Gaussian distribution \(\mathcal{N}(0, \Lambda_0^{-1})\). First, by the Woodbury Matrix identity, we'll find the covariance matrix \(\Sigma = \Lambda_0^{-1}\). We have: $\(\begin{equation} (A + UCV)^{-1} = A^{-1} - A^{-1} U (C^{-1} + V A^{-1} U)^{-1} V A^{-1} \end{equation}\)$

Define \(C = \frac{\Lambda^{-1}}{\sigma^4}\), \(U = - \bm{\Phi}, V = \bm{\Phi}^T, A = \frac{I}{\sigma^2}\). Then, we have: $\(\begin{equation} \Lambda_0^{-1} = \sigma^2 I - \sigma^2 I \bm{\Phi} (\sigma^4 \Lambda - \sigma^2 \bm{\Phi}^T \bm{\Phi})^{-1} \bm{\Phi}^T \sigma^2 I \end{equation}\)$

Substitute in \(\Lambda\), we get: $\(\begin{align} \Lambda_0^{-1} &= \sigma^2 I + \sigma^2 \bm{\Phi} (\sigma^4 (\frac{I}{\alpha^2} + \frac{\bm{\Phi}^T \bm{\Phi}}{\sigma^2}) - \sigma^2 \bm{\Phi}^T \bm{\Phi})^{-1} \bm{\Phi}^T \sigma^2 \\ &= \sigma^2 I + \sigma^4 \bm{\Phi}( \frac{\sigma^4}{\alpha^2} I + \sigma^2 \bm{\phi}^T \bm{\Phi} - \sigma^2 \bm{\Phi}^T \bm{\Phi})^{-1} \bm{\Phi}^T \\ &= \sigma^2 I + \sigma^4 \bm{\Phi} (\frac{\sigma^4}{\alpha^2} I)^{-1} \bm{\Phi}^T \\ &= \sigma^2 I + \alpha^2 \bm{\Phi} \bm{\Phi}^T \end{align}\)$

This is the covariance matrix for the unnormalized probability distribution in the numerator. We will now find it's determinant. By the matrix determinant lemma, we have:

\[\begin{equation} \det(A + UWV^T) = \det(W^{-1} + V^T A^{-1} U) \det(W) \det(A) \end{equation}$$ $$\begin{equation} \det(\sigma^2 I + \bm{\Phi} \alpha^2 I \bm{\Phi}^T) = \det(\frac{I}{\alpha^2} + \bm{\Phi}^T \frac{I}{\sigma^2} \bm{\Phi}) \det(\alpha^2 I) \det (\sigma^2 I) \end{equation}\]

Notice that \(\Lambda = \frac{I}{\alpha^2} + \frac{\bm{\Phi}^T \bm{\Phi}}{\sigma^2}\). So we can re-write: $\(\begin{equation} \det(\Lambda_0^{-1}) = \det(\Lambda) (\alpha^2)^D (\sigma^2)^N \end{equation}\)$ $\(\begin{equation} \label{eq:lambda_0} \det(\Lambda_0^{-1})^{\frac{1}{2}} = \det(\Lambda)^{\frac{1}{2}}(\alpha^2)^{\frac{D}{2}} (\sigma^2)^{\frac{N}{2}} \end{equation}\)$

Recall our expression for the marginal likelihood (re-written here).

\[\begin{equation} p(\bm{y} \mid X, \sigma^2, \alpha^2, \bm{z}) = \frac{ \exp \left \{ - \frac{1}{2} \bm{y}^T \left [ \frac{I}{\sigma^2} - \frac{ \bm{\Phi}}{\sigma^2} \Lambda^{-1} \frac{\bm{\Phi}^T}{\sigma^2} \right ] \bm{y} \right \} }{(2 \pi)^{\frac{N}{2}} (\sigma^2)^\frac{n}{2} \det (\Lambda)^{\frac{1}{2}} (\alpha^2)^{\frac{D}{2}}} \end{equation}\]

Substituting in our result from [eq:lambda_0]{reference-type="ref" reference="eq:lambda_0"}, we get:

\[\begin{equation} p(\bm{y} \mid X, \sigma^2, \alpha^2, \bm{z}) = \frac{ \exp \left \{ - \frac{1}{2} \bm{y}^T \Lambda_0 \bm{y} \right \} }{(2 \pi)^{\frac{N}{2}} \det(\Lambda_0^{-1})^{\frac{1}{2}}} \end{equation}\]

In other words, this is a fully normalized Gaussian distribution, i.e. \(\mathbf{y} \mid X, \sigma^2, \alpha^2, \bm{z} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \Lambda_0^{-1})\).

Define \(\bm{\Sigma}_0 = \Lambda_0^{-1}\) to be the covariance matrix of the marginal likelihood.

Thus, the log marginal likelihood is:

\[\begin{equation} \log p(\mathbf{y} \mid \mathbf{z}, X, \alpha^2, \sigma^2) = - \frac{1}{2} \mathbf{y}^T \bm{\Sigma}_0 \mathbf{y} - \frac{1}{2} \log (\det(\bm{\Sigma}_0^{-1})) - \frac{N}{2} \log (2 \pi) \end{equation}\]

This compartmentalizes nicely into a data fit and model complexity term.

The data fit term is \(\mathbf{y}^T \bm{\Sigma}_0 \mathbf{y}\) and the model complexity term is \(\log (\det(\bm{\Sigma}_0^{-1})\)). The data fit term measures the discrepancy between the data your model expects (in the covariance matrix) and the data itself (in \(\mathbf{y}\)). The determinant of the covariance matrix measures the volume of probability that can be explained by the model - so it penalizes more complex models. This is a built-in Occam's razor.

We can perform Marginal Likelihood Optimization or Type 2 MLE to learn hyperparameters - this is much more efficient than grid search where the number of runs grows exponentially with the number of hyperparameters. It's very useful but doesn't answer the exact question we want - it answers the likelihood of the data under all parameter settings when we want the probability of the model class itself.

docs.title: Predictive Distribution
docs.text:
Let's say we have optimized the marginal likelihood to obtain point estimates \(\hat{\alpha}^2, \hat{\sigma}^2\). Our ultimate goal is to make predictions. With frequentist statistics, we make point estimate predictions but with a Bayesian toolbox, we can construct an entire distribution of predictions. We want to find \(p(y_* \mid \mathcal{D}, x_*, \hat{\sigma}^2, \hat{\alpha}^2)\). We can marginalize over the weights.

\[ egin{equation} p(y_* \mid \mathcal{D}, x_*, \hat{\sigma}^2, \hat{\alpha}^2) = \int p(y_* \mid \mathbf{w}, x_*, \sigma^2) \ p(\mathbf{w} \mid \mathcal{D}, \hat{\alpha}^2) \ d\mathbf{w} \end{equation}\]

First, we need to find the posterior distribution. We have:

\[ egin{equation} p(\mathbf{w} \mid \mathcal{D}) \propto p(\mathcal{D} \mid \mathbf{w}) \ p(\mathbf{w} \mid \alpha) \end{equation}\]

where \(\mathbf{w} \mid \alpha \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \alpha^2 I)\). However, we already found the posterior distribution as the integrand in the marginal likelihood. We found that \(\mathbf{w} \mid \mathcal{D} \sim N( m{\mu}, \Lambda^{-1})\) where \( m{\mu} = \Lambda^{-1} rac{ m{\Phi}^T \mathbf{y}}{\sigma^2}\) and \(\Lambda = rac{I}{\alpha^2} + rac{ m{\Phi}^T m{\Phi}}{\sigma^2}\). In finding the predictive distribution, we can avoid computing the integral through the laws of total expectation and variance.

\[ egin{equation} E[y_* \mid x_*, \hat{\sigma}^2, \hat{\alpha}^2, \mathcal{D}] = E_{\mathbf{w} \mid \hat{\alpha}^2, \mathcal{D}}[E[y_* \mid \mathbf{w}, x_*, \hat{\sigma}]] \end{equation}$$ $$ egin{equation} E[y_* \mid \mathbf{w}, x_*, \hat{\sigma}] = E[\mathbf{w}^T \phi(x) + \mathcal{N}(0, \hat{\sigma}^2)] = \phi(x)^T E[\mathbf{w}] \end{equation}\]

From the posterior mean, we know that: $\( egin{equation} \phi(x)^T E[\mathbf{w}] = m{\mu}^T \phi(x) \end{equation}\)$

The expectation of a scalar is a scalar so this is the predictive mean. For the predictive variance, we have: $\( egin{equation} \mathop{\mathrm{Var}}(y_* \mid x_*, \hat{\alpha}^2, \hat{\sigma}^2, \mathcal{D}) = E_{\mathbf{w} \mid \hat{\alpha}^2, \mathcal{D}}[\mathop{\mathrm{Var}}(y_* \mid x_*, \hat{\sigma}^2, \mathbf{w})] + \mathop{\mathrm{Var}}(E_{\mathbf{w} \mid \hat{\alpha}^2, \mathcal{D}}[y_* \mid x_*, \mathbf{w}, \hat{\sigma}^2]) \end{equation}\)$

We have \(y_* \mid x_*, \hat{\sigma}^2, \mathbf{w} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \hat{\sigma}^2)\) so the variance is \(\hat{\sigma}^2\) so the expectation of the variance is just \(\hat{\sigma}^2\). The variance of the expectation is just \(\mathop{\mathrm{Var}}[E_{\mathbf{w} \mid \hat{\alpha}^2, \mathcal{D}}[\mathbf{w}^T \phi(x_*) + \epsilon(x)]]\). The expectation just becomes \(\mathbf{w}^T \phi(x_*)\). The variance of this term is thus \(\phi(x_*)^T \mathop{\mathrm{Var}}[\mathbf{w}] \phi(x_*)\). However, the variance of this term is just the covariance matrix so we have:

\[ egin{equation} y_* \mid x_*, \mathcal{D}, \hat{\sigma}^2, \hat{\alpha}^2 \sim \mathcal{N}(y(x_*) ; m_*, u_*) \end{equation}$$ $$ egin{equation} m_* = m{\mu}^T \phi(x) \end{equation}$$ $$ egin{equation} u_* = \hat{\sigma}^2 + \phi(x_*)^T \Lambda^{-1} \phi(x_*) \end{equation}\]

:::: centeredboxblue ::: example Example: 3. Sanity Check

\(m_* = m{\mu} ^T \phi(x)\): this is a reasonable answer for our mean as it's the inner product of our posterior mean (expected values of parameter after having observed \(\mathcal{D}\)) with our parameters. The variance \( u_* = \sigma^2 + \phi(x_*)^T \Lambda^{-1} \phi(x_*)\) so we have noise variance \(\sigma^2\) and the uncertainty over the parameters.

Consider the extreme. Suppose we have a ton of noise, i.e. \(\sigma o \infty\). Then, the posterior mean goes to \(0\) and variance goes to \(\alpha^2 I\). This is the prior. In other words, if there's a ton of noise and we can't infer information from the likelihood, our posterior becomes our prior. If \(\alpha o 0\), we have zero variance over weights and our distribution becomes peaked with mean \(0\) and variance \(0\). Our prior is too strong. ::: ::::

To summarize:

  1. We find the predictive distribution by marginalizing over different parameter settings and weighting them by the posterior probability. $\( egin{equation} p(y(x_*) \mid \alpha^2, \sigma^2) = \int p(y(x_*) \mid \mathbf{w}, \sigma^2) p(\mathbf{w} \mid \alpha^2) \ d\mathbf{w} \end{equation}\)$

  2. We optimize the hyperparameters by maximizing the marginal likelihood of the data. $\( egin{equation} \hat{\alpha}^2, \hat{\sigma}^2 = \mathop{\mathrm{arg\,max}}_{\alpha^2, \sigma^2} p(D \mid \alpha^2, \sigma^2) = \mathop{\mathrm{arg\,max}}_{\alpha^2, \sigma^2} \int p(D \mid w, \sigma^2) p(w \mid \alpha^2) \ d\mathbf{w} \end{equation}\)$

  3. We make predictions \(p(y(x_*) \mid \hat{\alpha}^2, \hat{\sigma}^2), x_*, \mathcal{D}\).

However, this does not constitute a fully Bayesian treatment. Fully Bayesian methods do not optimize hyperparameters but rather marginalize over them (Bayesian Model Average). We don't bet everything on a single setting of parameters because we have uncertainty over our parameters even after we've observed data. In this procedure, we're optimizing \(\hat{\sigma}^2, \hat{\alpha}^2\) and conditioning our predictive distribution on those values. But our posterior over \(\alpha^2\) shouldn't be a point mass but rather a distribution.

What we really want to find is \(p(y(x_*) \mid \mathbf{y}, X, x_*)\).

\[p(y(x_*) \mid \mathbf{y}, X, x_*) = \int p(y(x_*) \mid \alpha^2, \sigma^2) p(\alpha^2, \sigma^2 \mid D) \ d\alpha^2 \ d\sigma^2\]

We apply our reasoning for marginalizing over \(\mathbf{w}\) to also marginalize over \(\alpha^2, \sigma^2\). The posterior distribution over \(\alpha^2, \sigma^2\) is thus:

\[p(\alpha^2, \sigma^2 \mid D) \propto p(D \mid \alpha^2, \sigma^2) p(\alpha^2, \sigma^2)\]

The earlier \(3\)-step procedure is a reasonably Bayesian approach as it's doing the BMA over low-level parameters \(\mathbf{w}\) which are highly influential on our predictions. Optimizing the marginal likelihood also has a automatic model complexity penalty which has less risk of overfitting so it's not a completely non-Bayesian approach. This is called empirical Bayes.

The prior distribution has hyperparameters and we theoretically want to marginalize over those. However, this just constitutes moving one layer up a hierarchy of priors, hyperparameters for the priors, hyperparameters for those hyperparameters, etc. When do we stop doing integration? Isn't there infinite recursion? The intuitive answer is that at some point, it doesn't matter too much. If \(w\) are the parameters, \(\alpha^2, \sigma^2\) are the hyper-parameters, so on and so forth, the setting of those don't influence our predictions as much as we have so much uncertainty.
